era_context: The Late Roman Empire in the midst of civil war. The usurper Magnus Maximus, proclaimed emperor by troops in Britannia, challenged the legitimate emperors Theodosius I (East) and Valentinian II (West). This conflict would dominate the period until 388. In China, the Northern Wei Dynasty was consolidating power. In Mesoamerica, Classic Maya civilization was in advanced decline.

events.title: Magnus Maximus proclaimed emperor in Britannia; civil war begins
events.description: Magnus Maximus, a Roman general of Spanish origin, was proclaimed emperor by troops in Britannia. Maximus then invaded Gaul and defeated the Western Roman Emperor Gratian, establishing himself as a rival claimant to imperial power. This triggered a civil war that would last until 388 CE. Maximus represented a challenge to the legitimacy of Theodosius I and the young Valentinian II. The conflict revealed the fragility of imperial authority and the power of the military to make and unmake emperors. Maximus's challenge was ultimately unsuccessful, but it destabilized the Western Empire and contributed to the conditions that would lead to Germanic invasions.

sources.name: Ammianus Marcellinus, Roman History (though written earlier, covers this period indirectly)
Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans
Coins and inscriptions from the period

events.certainty_note: The proclamation of Maximus and the beginning of civil war are well-attested in multiple sources. The date 383 CE is secure.

events.title: Battle of the Frigidus (or Frigidus River) — preliminary conflicts
events.description: In 383, the first major military confrontation between Theodosius I and Magnus Maximus occurred near the Frigidus River in the Balkans. The exact details and outcome of this engagement are debated by historians, but it marked the beginning of direct military conflict between the two claimants. The battle was inconclusive, and the conflict would continue for several more years. The Frigidus campaign demonstrated the military resources both sides could mobilize and the stakes of the civil war.

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans

events.certainty_note: The Frigidus campaign is attested in sources, but the exact chronology and details are disputed. Some sources place major fighting in 388 rather than 383. The dating is uncertain.

events.title: Gratian assassinated in Lyons
events.description: The Western Roman Emperor Gratian, defeated by Magnus Maximus's forces, was assassinated in Lyons while attempting to flee. Gratian's death removed a legitimate claimant and consolidated Maximus's control over the Western Empire. The assassination was carried out by Maximus's supporters, though the exact circumstances are unclear. Gratian's death marked a significant shift in the balance of power and left the young Valentinian II as the nominal Western emperor, dependent on the support of Theodosius I.

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans
Ambrose of Milan, Letters (contemporary witness accounts)

events.certainty_note: Gratian's assassination is well-attested in multiple sources. The date 383 CE is secure.

events.title: Northern Wei Dynasty continues consolidation in northern China
events.description: The Northern Wei (Tuoba) Dynasty continued its process of consolidating control over northern China. In 383, the Wei were engaged in administrative reforms and military campaigns to expand their territorial base. This period saw the gradual sinicization of the Tuoba people and the adoption of Chinese governmental structures. The Wei were emerging as a major power that would eventually unify northern China.

sources.name: Wei Shu (History of the Wei Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Northern Wei consolidation is clear, but specific events in 383 are not well-documented in surviving sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact chronology of the civil war between Theodosius and Maximus is disputed. Some sources place the major military confrontations in 388 rather than 383. The Battle of the Frigidus may have occurred in 388 rather than 383. The circumstances of Gratian's assassination are unclear, and some sources suggest he may have died in battle rather than being assassinated. The significance of Maximus's challenge is sometimes overstated—he was ultimately defeated relatively quickly, and his reign did not fundamentally alter the trajectory of the empire.
historiographic_note: The record for 383 CE is dominated by Roman sources, with a strong bias toward political and military events. The sources are primarily from the Eastern Empire and later compilations. Chinese sources on Northern Wei are abundant but compiled centuries later. The perspectives of ordinary people, women, and non-elite groups are largely absent. The sources tend to emphasize the dramatic political conflict while obscuring economic and social changes.

graph_edges.note: Maximus's proclamation led to Gratian's assassination
The civil war initiated by Maximus lasted until 388
